summary: Gruber serves up 'claim chowder' on Motley Fool writer Anders Bylund, who predicted in March 2010 that HP and competitors would kill the iPad, and two months earlier named Apple as one of the worst stocks of 2010. Bylund argued the iPad was merely an oversized iPod Touch that would be overwhelmed by Flash-supporting competitors. He also claimed Apple's stock was overpriced through a 'Reality Distortion Field.' Gruber methodically shows how every one of Bylund's predictions proved spectacularly wrong — the iPad became a killer product, the Mac gained share, and Apple's stock nearly doubled from $200 to $366.
key_points: Bylund predicted a 'flash flood' of competing tablets would kill the iPad, citing Flash support and multitasking as decisive advantages — none of which materialized
The HP tablet Bylund pinned his hopes on wasn't even the TouchPad but the Windows 7 slate Ballmer demoed at CES 2010, which never became a real product
Bylund named Apple as one of the worst stocks of 2010 when it traded at $200; it closed at $366 by August 2011
Every specific claim Bylund made — iPad limited to Apple faithful, Mac share stalling, iPhone facing serious competition — turned out to be the opposite of what happened
money_quotes: Of course, the HP tablet he thought was going to kill the iPad wasn't the TouchPad, but rather the Windows 7-running 'slate' that Steve Ballmer heralded at CES in 2010.
But it's not like Bylund has ever done better.
At the time, Apple's stock price was around $200 a share.
Since then, the 'iSlate' has become a killer product, the Mac has continued to steal market share, and the iPhone 4 is the top-selling handset in the world.
key_insight: The consensus view that iPad competitors with Flash and multitasking would overwhelm Apple proved not just wrong but comically so, as every specific prediction — product, stock, and market share — went in the opposite direction.
